title: Planning a Smallholding Layout from Scratch
subtitle: Thinking through species separation, paddock rotation, machinery access, shelter, and water
excerpt: A good smallholding layout saves time, reduces disease pressure, and keeps your animals comfortable. Planning before you put up fencing or build shelters prevents costly mistakes. This tutorial walks through the key decisions.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from general smallholding practice references including Starting and Running a Smallholding (Liz Shankland); AHDB paddock management guidance; DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock (various species). No single primary source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A grazing management system where animals are moved between paddocks in a planned sequence, allowing grazed paddocks to rest and recover before use again.

The most frequent wind direction at a given location. In the UK, the prevailing wind is typically from the south-west. Siting shelters and housing to block the prevailing wind reduces heat loss and stress in livestock.
Keeping different livestock species in separate areas to reduce cross-contamination of parasites and disease, and to manage different grazing behaviours.
Any fixed location where livestock can access fresh drinking water. May be a trough, automatic drinker, or natural water source that has been made safe and fenced.

content.text: Start on paper, not with a fence post

A scaled sketch of your land takes an hour and saves thousands of pounds. Fence posts are hard to move. Buildings are harder. Draw out your holding first. Mark existing trees, buildings, slopes, boggy areas, and access gates. Work around what you have.
Think in systems, not individual animals. Plan for the number of animals you aim to run at full capacity, not just your first batch. Fencing a field once is much cheaper than fencing it twice.
Species separation
Species separation

is one of the most valuable things you can plan for. Different species share some parasites but not others. Rotating cattle and sheep on the same ground breaks parasite cycles. However, running pigs with poultry increases disease risk. Separate pig areas from poultry areas permanently if possible.

Consider behaviour differences too. Goats escape through or over fences that hold sheep. Cattle break fences that hold horses. Each species needs fencing suited to it. Design your paddock boundaries with the most challenging species in mind.
Paddock rotation
A single large field with permanent animals degrades quickly. Soil compaction, worm burden, and overgrazing all increase.
Paddock rotation

solves this. Divide your grazing land into at least three paddocks. Use one, rest two. Move animals when grass is grazed down to around 5 centimetres. Let paddocks rest for at least six weeks before regrazing.

The more paddocks you have, the more flexibility you gain. Four or five paddocks per species group is a practical target for a smallholding. Temporary electric fencing allows you to subdivide larger areas cheaply and change the layout as you learn your land.
Access for machinery

Think about how you will get a tractor, trailer, or feed delivery vehicle to every part of your holding. A paddock that can only be reached through another paddock is a problem. You will need to move animals, open gates, and cross grazed ground with heavy machinery at inconvenient times.

Plan a track or gateway system that allows vehicle access to each area without going through another. This track does not need to be surfaced at first. A worn earth track between gateways works well in dry weather. Consider a hardcore or gravel surface for areas used heavily in winter.
Gate width matters. A standard farm gate is 3 metres wide. A large round baler or muck spreader may need 3.5 to 4 metres of clearance. Measure your equipment before you fix gateposts.
Shelter from the prevailing wind
In the UK the
prevailing wind
usually comes from the south-west. Site buildings and field shelters so that the solid back wall faces south-west. Animals will naturally use the sheltered side.

Natural shelter is often better than built shelter. A thick hedge or belt of trees breaks wind over a long distance and also provides shade in summer. Preserve existing hedgerows wherever possible. They take decades to grow and are harder to replace than a building.
Avoid siting housing in a valley bottom or hollow. Cold air sinks into hollows and wet ground follows. Animals in low-lying housing are more prone to respiratory illness.
Water points
Every paddock needs access to a
water point

. Carrying water by hand across a field twice a day is exhausting and unsustainable. Plan the route for water supply pipework before fences go in. Burying a water pipe under a gateway is straightforward before posts are set. Retrofitting means digging up what you have built.
Site troughs away from gateways. Animals congregate around troughs and gateways independently. If they are in the same place, you get poaching of the soil and a muddy mess in winter.

Provide enough trough space for your stocking density. The rule of thumb is 6 to 10 linear centimetres per animal. A dominant animal blocking a single small trough causes dehydration in lower-ranked animals. This goes unnoticed until condition scores drop.
Putting it together

A good smallholding layout does not need to be complex. It needs to be practical. When you are exhausted and it is dark and wet, you want clear tracks, obvious gates, and water troughs that fill automatically. Design for your worst day, not your best.

Visit other small-scale holdings before you build. Every keeper learns things the hard way and most are happy to share what they would do differently. A few hours talking to someone with five years of experience on their land is worth more than any book.
